query: effectiveness of topical ropivacaine in reducing post-tonsillectomy morbidity in pediatric patients

retrieved_docs.title: Ropivacaine -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric patients: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends immediate availability of a 20% lipid emulsion for local anesthetic toxicity if a high dosage of amide anesthetics like ropivacaine is suspected in systemic vasculature. Topical local anesthetics are encouraged, but practitioners should avoid excessive doses on mucosal surfaces to prevent systemic uptake and possible toxicity (seizures, methemoglobinemia) while following the manufacturer’s surface area application guidelines. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetics in Children -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Next, one must know the local anesthetic that has been given to appropriately monitor for adequate analgesic effect and maintain vigilance for signs and symptoms of adverse effects and LAST.  Institutions should develop a standard handoff that details the local anesthetic used, the method and timing of the administration of the local anesthetic, and the expected analgesic effect and duration. Signs and symptoms of toxicity should be reviewed. Prompt recognition and treatment of LAST can prevent potentiation of LAST. [Level 1]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Regional Anesthesia -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Ideal body weight is used for the calculation of the maximal allowable dose of local anesthetic. This is 3mg/kg for bupivacaine, levobupivacaine, and ropivacaine, 5 mg/kg for lidocaine without epinephrine, and 7 mg/kg for lidocaine with epinephrine for bolus dosing. Toxicities of different local anesthetic drugs are additive. If a maximal dose of one LA has already been used, a different LA should not be used. Infants less than 6 months require at least a 30% reduction in the maximal doses due to increased free fractions and decreased elimination. An epidural infusion dose of 0.4 to 0.5 mg/kg/hr is used for bupivacaine (0.25%), levobupivacaine (0.25%), and ropivacaine (0.2%); this is reduced to 0.25 mg/kg/hr for infants less than 6 months. Chloroprocaine (2%) is a rapid, short-acting ester LA used in infants at a dose of 8 to 10 mg/kg/hr. It is rapidly metabolized and eliminated by plasma esterases. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Circumcision -- Clinical Significance -- Benefits
retrieved_docs.content: Locally injected anesthesia, oral sucrose solutions, topical analgesics such as lidocaine cream 4% (LMX-4) , and lidocaine/prilocaine cream can effectively minimize any pain without a regional or general anesthetic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tonsillitis -- Treatment / Management -- American Academy of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery, 2019 Clinical Practice Guideline: Tonsillectomy in Children (Update)
retrieved_docs.content: This updated guideline delivers evidence-based recommendations for tonsillectomy in pediatric patients. Preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative management, focusing on indications such as recurrent throat infections and obstructive sleep-disordered breathing, are addressed. The guideline also highlights the importance of shared decision-making between clinicians and caregivers.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetics in Children -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Prilocaine alone or as EMLA cream can cause methemoglobinemia, especially in neonates and infants less than 3 months old. The risk of methemoglobinemia is increased by concurrent medication use with medications such as dapsone, acetaminophen, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, phenytoin, phenobarbital, and chloroquine. Treatment of methemoglobinemia is supportive with oxygen and intravenous fluids, and if needed, IV methylene blue 1 to 2 mg/kg infused over 5 minutes or ascorbic acid. [6] [13] Methylene blue may be repeated every 30-60 minutes, not to exceed the maximum dose of 7 mg/kg. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetic Toxicity -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple case reports highlight pediatric LAST following the use of oral viscous lidocaine and EMLA cream with topical tetracaine. Providing education for parents and healthcare providers may aid in prevention, facilitate early recognition, and enhance outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Regional Anesthesia -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Regional analgesic techniques are indicated whenever possible for the management of postoperative pain. Several regional techniques are also used to treat various cancer and non-cancer chronic pain conditions. Regional techniques form an integral part of several enhanced recovery protocols and aid in minimizing the requirements of opioids to control postoperative pain. Adequate management of acute pain is essential to prevent the transition to chronic pain. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetics in Children -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline indications for the use of local anesthetics. Review the mechanism of action of local anesthetics and discuss their metabolism. Explain the risks contributing to the adverse effects and toxicity of local anesthetics, and explain local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) and its treatment. Identify the interprofessional team strategies for improving the care of and communication about local anesthetic administration and monitoring in children. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Consideration in Pierre Robin Sequence -- Clinical Significance -- Induction and Airway Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with PRS have preexisting airway obstruction, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), chronic hypoxia, and increased opioid sensitivity making them increasingly prone to develop postoperative respiratory complications. Prolonged surgical duration and pressure exerted on the base of the tongue by retractors and airway edema may worsen the preexisting obstruction. [19] The development of postoperative complications can be minimized by performing neck extension and jaw-thrust alone or using nasopharyngeal/ oropharyngeal airways. Post-operatively, nursing the child in a lateral position with neck extended can also reduce the possible complications. [12] To prevent postoperative respiratory obstruction, non-opioid analgesics, regional blocks, and local infiltration should be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Tonsillectomy and Adenoidectomy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The following guidelines are from the Clinical Practice Guideline: Tonsillectomy in Children from the American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery:

retrieved_docs.title: Tonsillectomy and Adenoidectomy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should counsel caregivers about tonsillectomy as a means to improve health in children with abnormal polysomnography who also have tonsil hypertrophy and sleep-disordered breathing. [Level II] Clinicians should counsel caregivers and explain that SDB may persist or recur after tonsillectomy and may require further management. [Level II] Clinicians should administer a single, intraoperative dose of intravenous dexamethasone to children undergoing tonsillectomy. [Level I] Clinicians should not routinely administer or prescribe perioperative antibiotics to children undergoing tonsillectomy. [Level I] The clinician should advocate for pain management after tonsillectomy and educate caregivers about the importance of managing and reassessing pain. [Level II] Clinicians who perform tonsillectomy should determine their rate of primary and secondary post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage at least annually. [Level II] " [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetics in Children -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Amide LA administration itself can lead to the risk of adverse effects and toxicity. Mixing different amide LAs together can result in a cumulative dose that may not be safe. A large volume of amide LA can affect uptake and accumulation, exceeding safe dose levels. Less invasive topical LA may be considered safer, but careful consideration of dosing must be taken to avoid an overdose. Higher lipophilicity of an amide LA can also increase the risk of toxicity by increasing the potency and duration of action. Finally, direct intravascular injection of LAs can cause adverse effects and toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics are a pharmacologic class employed to achieve reversible loss of sensation in a specific region of the body. Within this class, mepivakain, prilokain, and propoxykain are identified as child agents of the broader local anesthetic category, while bupivakain and etidokain are also classified as local anesthetics. Recognizing that mepivakain, prilokain, propoxykain, bupivakain, and etidokain share the same fundamental mechanism of action enables clinicians to select appropriate agents for regional blocks, infiltration, or topical anesthesia and to appreciate their related pharmacologic properties, including variations in potency, duration of effect, and safety profile.

In addition, Injection of locally-acting therapeutic substance into trachea is also categorized as a throat surgery. Both interventions involve therapeutic injections within the upper airway and represent distinct subtypes of throat surgery, illustrating the variety of surgical options available for managing laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: Local Anesthetic [EPC] is directly associated with local anesthetic throat preparations, indicating that the category of local anesthetic identified by the EPC classification encompasses formulations specifically designed to provide topical anesthesia of the throat. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse class of pharmacologic agents used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. This class includes chloralosa, chloroform, enfluran, etomidát, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. Each of these agents functions as a “child” of the broader category of general anesthetics, reflecting their role as specific drugs that share the common purpose of producing and maintaining general anesthesia. While all serve the same overarching clinical goal, they differ in chemical structure, mechanism of action, potency, onset and duration of effect, and side‑effect profile, allowing clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to the individual patient’s physiological status, surgical requirements, and risk factors. Understanding the full spectrum of these agents enhances patient safety, optimizes peri‑operative management, and supports informed selection of the most appropriate anesthetic regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 171

Local anesthetic throat preparations include products such as dyclonine and are associated with the entry “Local Anesthetic [EPC]”, reflecting their use in throat‑directed anesthesia. Together, these entities and relationships form a hierarchical framework that integrates chemical structure, therapeutic indication, and dosage form for topical and local anesthetic agents, supporting diverse clinical applications in pain relief, mucosal anesthesia, and dermatologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 95
retrieved_docs.content: doxepin as a topical antipruritic agent. Collectively, this network positions lidokain as a central agent in topical pain control, antipruritic therapy and mucosal anesthesia, often combined with capsaicin, menthol, methyl salicylate, hydrocortisone, pramoxine and epinephrine. Benoxinate and prokain illustrate derivation and parent‑child relationships within local anesthetic families, while chinin provides a deeper hierarchical link. Dyclonine and “Local Anesthetic [EPC]” represent specific throat‑preparation members, and the topical antihistamines (klemastin, chloropyramine, chlorphenoxamine, tripelenamin) together with doxepin expand the therapeutic scope to allergic and pruritic skin conditions. This integrated classification aids clinicians in selecting appropriate single agents or combination products for analgesia, anesthesia, anti‑inflammatory and antipruritic indications across oral, ocular, dermal and mucosal sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics are organized under the broader class anestetika, a subclass of the general “local anesthetics” category, and are contraindicated in patients receiving isokarboxazid. Within anestetika, specific agents such as benzokain (benzocaine), dyclonine, lidokain (lidocaine) and phenoxyethanol are defined as members (“isa”). Lidokain belongs to the phenylamide subclass, is a child of the local anesthetic group, and is listed as a member of local anesthetic throat preparations; it serves as the active moiety in several combination topical products that also contain kapsaicin, menthol and methyl‑salicylate (including multiple ointments, a medicated patch and various lotions), and appears as an inactive ingredient in one formulation, illustrating its versatile role in product design. Benzokain is likewise classified as a local anesthetic throat preparation, is linked to the synonym “fastins,” and functions as an inactive ingredient in capsaicin‑menthol‑methyl‑salicylate topical

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 741
retrieved_docs.content: Benzokain belongs to the hierarchy of local anesthetics, whose commercial formulations list capsaicin, menthol and methyl‑salicylate as inactive ingredients. Capsaicin, a biologically active toxin derived from the Capsicum plant of the Solanaceae family, is a key component of several topical products—including a 0.25 mg/mL capsaicin/100 mg/mL menthol/300 mg/mL methyl‑salicylate lotion, the Dendracin Neurodendraxcin preparation, and a 0.75 mg/mL capsaicin cream—and is classified under “substances affecting the sensory system” for its modulation of sensory nerve activity. Local anesthetics, a subset of sensory‑system‑affecting substances, include child agents such as mepivakain, prilokain and propoxykain, with related compounds ethylchloride and fenazopyridin also noted. Bupivakain and etidokain are additional local anesthetics used to achieve reversible loss of sensation in surgical and procedural settings. Carbapenems (karbapenemy), a structural class of β‑lactam antibiotics, encompass agents such as doripenem and ertapenem; doripenem is linked to bacterial carbapenem‑resistance mechanisms identified in rectal cultures, while ertapenem is contraindicated when co‑administered with local anesthetics, highlighting a drug‑interaction concern. Alantoin appears as an inactive ingredient in the capsaicin 0.75 mg/mL topical cream, serving a formulation role rather than therapeutic effect. Fenazopyridin exerts renal/urological activity alteration and is employed as a urinary analgesic to relieve discomfort from urinary tract irritation. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how benzokain‑based anesthetic products incorporate capsaicin‑derived ingredients, how capsaicin fits into sensory‑modulating toxin classifications, and how carbapenem antibiotics intersect with bacterial resistance patterns and anesthetic safety, informing clinical decisions in anesthesia, antimicrobial therapy, neuropathic pain management, and symptomatic relief of urinary tract symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 349
retrieved_docs.content: The concept “anestetika” is classified within analgesics and anesthetics and includes parent compounds methoxyfluran and the broader group “local anesthetics”. It is linked via isa relationships to pregnenolon, benzokain, dyclonine, and phenoxyethanol, indicating these substances are individual anesthetic members. Benzokain is further defined as a child of local anesthetics, belongs to the subgroup “local anesthetic throat preparations”, and is part of the general category “throat preparations”. Benzokain appears as an inactive ingredient in the topical formulations “capsaicin 0.25 MG/ML / menthol 100 MG/ML / methyl salicylate 300 MG/ML Topical Lotion” and the same product marketed as “[Dendracin Neurodendraxcin]”. These products contain the active agent kapsaicin (capsaicin), which is a parent of the Solanaceae alkaloids (“alkaloidy Solanaceí”), is classified as a biological toxin and as a substance affecting the sensory system, and is also the principal ingredient of “capsaicin 0.75 MG/ML Topical Cream”. Ethylchlorid is identified as a parent of the class “local anesthetics”, a class that modulates the sensory system. Specific local anesthetic agents within this class include mepivakain, prilokain, propoxykain, bupivakain, and etidokain, with bupivakain and etidokain explicitly noted as members. The entry “Local Anesthetic [EPC]” is associated with formulations for throat application, termed “local anesthetic throat preparations”, of which dyclonine is a member. Together, these relationships form a hierarchical network linking broad sensory‑modulating categories to individual anesthetic compounds, specialized throat preparations, and sensory‑active alkaloids, providing clinicians with insight for safe prescribing, formulation selection, and management of sensory‑related side effects.
